region_of_origin: East Asia; widely consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 229954003 refers to adzuki bean as a substance; no specific cooked or boiled form code is available in SNOMED CT. closest_parent used as the match type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled red beans is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), as commonly consumed as a side dish, dessert base, or snack in Singapore and across East and Southeast Asia.

nutrient_source.title: Red beans, boiled — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled red bean (F09020011). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 29 (±4) for boiled adzuki beans (red beans) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (29 × 24.77 × 1.5) / 100 = 10.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (8 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (532 mg/100 g) with high dietary fibre (7.3 g/100 g) — an outstanding DASH-diet profile
hypertension.guidance: Boiled red beans are highly suitable for hypertension management; their negligible sodium content (8 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (532 mg/100 g) support vasodilation and blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary guidelines — consume plain, unsweetened, and without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 29) driven by high dietary fibre (7.3 g/100 g) and moderate protein (7.52 g/100 g), resulting in slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled red beans are an excellent carbohydrate choice for Type 2 Diabetes management; with a GI of 29 and 7.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, they produce a blunted postprandial glucose response consistent with ADA and Diabetes Singapore guidelines — consume only unsweetened preparations and avoid red bean paste or sweetened red bean soup.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high soluble dietary fibre (7.3 g/100 g) supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting cardiac function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled red beans are cardioprotective; their negligible fat and cholesterol content, combined with high soluble fibre (7.3 g/100 g) that reduces LDL cholesterol and high potassium (532 mg/100 g) that supports cardiac rhythm, make them a recommended legume for cardiovascular disease prevention per WHO and AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (7.3 g/100 g) supporting gut microbiome-mediated immune regulation and antioxidant polyphenols (anthocyanins, proanthocyanidins) with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled red beans may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients through their high fibre content supporting gut microbiome diversity and their anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory activity — consume only unsweetened preparations, as added sugar is pro-inflammatory, and monitor individual legume tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled red bean (红豆, Kacang merah rebus) refers to adzuki beans (Vigna angularis) that have been soaked and boiled in plain water until soft and tender, without any added sugar, salt, or flavourings. The cooked beans are deep red-brown in colour with a soft, slightly mealy texture and a mild, sweet, earthy flavour. Red beans are a staple legume across East and Southeast Asia, widely consumed in Singapore, China, Japan, and Korea as a savoury side dish, a dessert base (e.g., red bean soup or tong sui), or a filling for pastries and confections. The plain boiled form assessed here is the unsweetened, minimally processed preparation. Nutritionally, boiled red beans are a high-fibre (7.3 g/100 g), moderate-protein (7.52 g/100 g), very low-fat (0.1 g/100 g) food with a low glycemic index of 29, making them one of the most metabolically favourable legumes for blood glucose management. Their potassium content (532 mg/100 g) is exceptionally high and sodium is negligible (8 mg/100 g), giving them an outstanding DASH-diet profile for hypertension. The high soluble fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction and cardiovascular health. Red beans are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. They are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed in the plain boiled form without added sugar or salt.

Are red beans good for heart health?
meal_context: ingredient in red bean soup (tong sui)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F09020011) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 29 for boiled adzuki beans is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI are supported by named approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrients, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled red bean (F09020011) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g (0.03 g) and sugar_g (0.5 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for adzuki beans cooked (NDB 16001) as the closest approved secondary source, consistent with the HPB total fat value of 0.1 g/100 g. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — red beans are a plant-based food and naturally contain no cholesterol. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (GI 29 × carbohydrate 24.77 g × 1.5) / 100 = 10.7 g. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input has been noted; while red beans are whole legumes, the standard classification is 'whole legume' rather than 'wholegrain' — this has been preserved in dietary flags as plant-based and high-fibre. This record covers only the plain unsweetened boiled preparation; sweetened red bean preparations (red bean paste, red bean soup with sugar) have substantially different nutritional profiles and are not covered by this record. SNOMED CT match type set to 'closest_parent' as code 229954003 refers to adzuki bean as a substance with no specific cooked form available.
